source.title: On the Wrong Track: Process and Content in Moral Psychology.

confidence_rationale: Philosophical critique of Greene's dual-process model of moral judgment. Relevant to MECH-025b because the precision-responsibility bridge implicitly assumes a process->content mapping similar to Greene's (high precision/deliberation -> higher responsibility). Kahane argues this mapping is empirically unsupported, that controlled processing in 'utilitarian' judgment may actually reflect moral deliberation independent of utilitarian content. Tempers MECH-025b's bridge by warning against tying processing mode directly to moral conclusions.
failure_signatures: If MECH-025b's bridge committed to a strong process->content mapping (high precision -> particular moral verdict), Kahane's critique would directly apply. The bridge actually committed to is weaker (precision -> responsibility weight), but the warning still applies to extensions that would tie precision to specific moral verdicts.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Greene's dual-process model ties automatic processing to deontological judgment and controlled processing to utilitarian judgment. Kahane argues this tie is based on misinterpretation of evidence; controlled processing in apparently utilitarian judgments reflects general moral deliberation, not utilitarian reasoning specifically. Neuroimaging shows similar correlates for intuitive and counterintuitive judgments regardless of utilitarian/deontological content.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-025b's precision-responsibility bridge assumes that the precision regime under which an action is committed is the right diagnostic for responsibility weight. Kahane's critique cautions that processing-mode-to-moral-content mappings tend to be empirically fragile. For MECH-025b, this implies the bridge should commit only to responsibility weight (a structural property), not to which moral verdict obtains (which is content-level). The current MECH-025b statement is consistent with Kahane's caution; future extensions that tie precision to particular moral conclusions would risk the failure mode he describes.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Kahane's target is Greene specifically, not MECH-025b. The relevance to MECH-025b is indirect: it constrains how the bridge can be elaborated, not whether the basic bridge holds. Kahane is also a philosophical paper (Mind & Language), not empirical -- though it cites and re-interprets neuroimaging.
mapping.source_context: Philosophical analysis paper; cites and re-interprets Greene's neuroimaging studies.

confidence_components.notes: Strong philosophical paper; relevance to MECH-025b is indirect (constraint on extensions, not direct test). The caveat is real and worth incorporating.
